JavaScript Core (18) — Operators, Types & Grammar: Truthy and Falsy
JavaScript
Operators, Types & Grammar

Truthy and falsy values show up most often inside conditionals — and that's exactly where they hide a bunch of traps. In this post we look at why Truthy/Falsy isn't really the same thing as implicit coercion, how to read it off a truth table, and why you're already using this trick every day without realizing it.
/en/javascript/2020/06/28/javascript-core-17-loose-strict-equality-coercion/
JavaScript Core (17): Loose Equality, Strict Equality, and Implicit Coercion

Strictly speaking, JavaScript is kind of a nosy language — it loves to convert your types for you whether you asked it to or not. This post walks through loose equality (==), strict equality (===), and the implicit coercion that happens behind the scenes, with the MDN conversion table to back it up.
/en/javascript/2020/06/28/javascript-core-16-operator-precedence-associativity/
JavaScript Core Concepts (16) — Operators, Types & Grammar: Precedence and Associativity

We covered operators last time, so now let's talk about two things that trip people up: precedence and associativity. We'll walk through how JavaScript actually evaluates an arithmetic expression, why 1 < 2 < 3 and 3 > 2 > 1 give surprising results, and the sneaky expression behavior behind chained assignments like a = b = 1.
/en/javascript/2020/06/28/javascript-core-15-operators-types-grammar-operators/
JavaScript Core Concepts (15): Operators Are Basically Tiny Functions
Operators, Types and Grammar

Next up: operators. You use them constantly no matter what you're building. This post covers what an operator actually is, why every operator is really just a tiny function under the hood, the operand counts (unary, binary, ternary), and what infix, prefix, and postfix expressions mean.
/en/javascript/2020/06/21/javascript-core-14-primitive-and-object-types/
JavaScript Core Concepts (14): Primitive Types vs. Object Types
Operators, Types, and Grammar

The clearer you are on how types work in JavaScript, the easier real-world development gets. This post walks through the seven primitive types, the wrapper objects behind them, the famous typeof null bug, and why you should avoid declaring primitives with wrapper objects like new String().
/en/javascript/2020/06/21/javascript-core-13-dynamic-typing/
JavaScript Core Concepts (13): Dynamic Typing — Why You Never Declare Types

When you write JavaScript, you never have to declare what type a variable is. The reason is simple — JavaScript is a dynamically typed language. In this post I'll walk through what dynamic typing actually means, how it differs from a language like C, and the two type-conversion traps (explicit and implicit) that love to bite you.
/en/javascript/2020/06/13/javascript-core-12-asi-automatic-semicolon-insertion/
JavaScript Core (12): How Can Semicolons Be Optional? Meet ASI

How come JavaScript sometimes needs semicolons and sometimes doesn't? That's down to a mechanism called ASI — Automatic Semicolon Insertion. It's handy, but it can also bite you in surprising ways. This post walks through what ASI is, where it silently breaks your code, and the rules you should keep in mind.
/en/javascript/2020/06/07/javascript-core-11-statements-and-expressions/
JavaScript Core Concepts (11): Statements vs Expressions — The One Everybody Mixes Up

Statements vs expressions is one of those concepts almost everyone gets fuzzy on — me included. In this post we untangle the difference: why a variable declaration counts as a statement even though it has an equals sign, why expressions always return a value, and how function declarations, function expressions, blocks and object literals fit into the picture.
/en/javascript/2020/06/06/javascript-core-10-execution-context-scope-exercises/
JavaScript Core Concepts (10): Execution Context & Scope — Practice Quiz
Execution Context & Scope

We've finally reached the last post in the series — a practice quiz. Most of these questions I wrote myself, but they're still well worth running through to lock in everything we've covered. One tip: don't just paste the code into a console and run it. Trace through it in your head first and figure out the answer by reasoning about what should happen.
/en/javascript/2020/05/31/javascript-core-09-threads-sync-async/
JavaScript Core Concepts (9): Threads, Sync, and Async — Why Order Matters
Execution Context and Scope

This is where we get into one of the most important ideas in JavaScript — the whole sync vs async, single-threaded thing that trips so many people up. We'll start with what a thread even is, why JavaScript can only do one thing at a time, and how the Event queue lets it handle async work anyway.
